danger_signals.visual_form: ball speed reduction and reduced steering control on-screen
danger_signals.meaning: pitcher weakening, vulnerability to hits increases

safety_signals.meaning: team advantage; mercy rule (10+ run lead) triggers game end

safety_signals.visual_form: pitcher substitution with full stamina visual state

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: 8 teams differentiated by location name only (Boston, California, Detroit, Houston, Minnesota, New York, St. Louis, San Francisco)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: scoreboard displays competing team location; roster composition reflects real 1986-1987 playoff teams

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: live scoreboard with running score, inning counter, runner positions on base paths
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: players animate batting, pitching, fielding; score updates in real-time

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: visual slowdown of pitch speed; reduced ball trajectory control indicators
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: pitcher performance degrades; reliever substitution becomes necessary

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: full stamina visual state; sharp ball movement control
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: high pitch velocity; precise steering response

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: visual representation of occupied bases on field display
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: runner behavior changes (stealing attempts, advancement risk)

environmental_storytelling.example: each location-team reflects actual 1986-1987 playoff rosters; player names and abilities encode real-world baseball context

Vince Coleman shown as fastest (difficult to catch stealing), Nolan Ryan and Roger Clemens as fastest pitchers, Fernando Valenzuela with pitch movement instead of speed—player identity communicated through gameplay stats
American League and National League all-star rosters feature established veterans and up-and-coming stars; roster cross-section communicates competitive tier and era context
rudimentary box score displayed during and after game showing composite statistics per team; quantitative visual summary of match state

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: high pitch speed, full steering control, high stamina available
reduced pitch speed, reduced steering control, low stamina
very high base-running speed; difficult to catch stealing second base
reduced advancement speed; easier to throw out attempting extra bases
high ability for solid contact; high power output when contact made
high ability to make contact; variable power output

notes: R.B.I. Baseball (1988 NES) uses minimal visual differentiation between factions due to lack of MLB licensing; teams identified purely by location name. World state communication relies heavily on: (1) scoreboard numerics and inning display, (2) stamina/fatigue visual feedback on pitchers, (3) base runner positioning, and (4) box score statistics. No team colors, logos, or symbolic markers present. Player identity communicated through name display and gameplay stat differences (speed, power, pitch movement). The mercy rule (10+ run lead) is a key world-state threshold that immediately ends game. Environmental storytelling achieved through roster accuracy and player attribute matching (e.g., Vince Coleman's speed). Faction distinction is minimal and functional rather than aesthetic.
